topic.what: Barry Marshall ingested a culture of Helicobacter pylori (then Campylobacter pyloridis) and developed acute gastritis, fulfilling Koch's third postulate for bacterial causation of gastritis.
topic.how: Marshall consumed a broth culture of H. pylori isolated from a patient with gastritis. Endoscopy on day 10 confirmed histological gastritis with the organism present; symptoms resolved after antibiotic treatment.
topic.why_matters: This experiment provided the critical causal proof that a bacterium causes gastritis, completing the chain of evidence that would ultimately overturn the stress-acid paradigm and lead to the 2005 Nobel Prize.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Degree to which Koch's postulates were satisfied for H. pylori causing gastritis
characteristics.value: Postulates 1-3 fulfilled: organism isolated from disease, grown in pure culture, disease reproduced upon inoculation
characteristics.vs_baseline: Prior evidence was associational only (Marshall & Warren 1984)
characteristics.evidence: Single-subject self-experimentation with pre/post endoscopy and histology
characteristics.context: Self-experimentation by Barry Marshall, Royal Perth Hospital, 1984

characteristics.what_it_measures: Days from ingestion to endoscopic confirmation of gastritis
characteristics.value: Symptoms began day 5-8; endoscopy confirmed gastritis on day 10
characteristics.vs_baseline: No prior data on acute H. pylori infection timeline
characteristics.evidence: Serial clinical observation and day-10 endoscopy

good_for: Definitive proof of H. pylori as cause of gastritis
Historical model for fulfilling Koch's postulates
Teaching scientific methodology and ethics of self-experimentation

novelty: First and only successful human self-inoculation experiment proving bacterial causation of gastritis
Fulfilled Koch's postulates for H. pylori — previously thought impossible for gastric pathogens
Demonstrated that acute H. pylori infection progresses rapidly to histological gastritis
Showed antibiotic treatment could resolve the infection
open_problems: Long-term consequences of acute infection not studied in this case
Ethical framework for self-experimentation remains debated
Mechanism of pathogenesis (urease, CagA, VacA) not yet identified
